sound_type: rapid chipping twittering -- high-pitched continuous stream of chip notes at 5000-8000 Hz, 5-10 chips/second; individually quiet but flocks of thousands create immersive wall of sound; E=4
ecosystem: Aerial -- spends nearly entire life in flight. Breeds in old-growth snags and chimneys. Communal chimney roosts (Chapman Elementary, Portland: up to 35,000 birds). Summer breeder. Migratory to Central America/northern South America.
season: Summer breeder in PNW. Peak roost spectacle mid-September. Migratory. Departs September-October, returns April-May.
family: Apodidae (FIRST -- NEW FAMILY. Swifts enter the engine.)

coordinates.description: Chapman Elementary School, Portland, OR -- largest known Vaux's Swift roost. Species breeds throughout PNW in old-growth forests and chimneys.

taxonomy_note.description: First Vaux's Swift entry. FIRST APODIDAE -- new family enters. Third new family in four degrees (Strigidae > Caprimulgidae > Apodidae). First genus: Chaetura. Communal chimney roost spectacle. Life on the wing. Near-vestigial feet.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Chaetura from Greek 'chaite' (bristle/hair) + 'oura' (tail) -- referring to the stiff, spiny tail feathers used for clinging. vauxi for William S. Vaux, Philadelphia naturalist.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Apodiformes. First entry. Swift order (also includes hummingbirds).
taxonomy_note.family_position: FIRST APODIDAE. Third new family in four degrees. Swifts enter.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Chipping: 5000-8000 Hz. High-pitched. Individual chips very brief (0.02-0.05s).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual chip: 0.02-0.05 seconds. Continuous during flight. Roost entry: 20-45 minutes of collective twittering.
song_metrics.note_structure: RAPID CHIPPING: Continuous stream of brief, high-pitched chip notes. 5-10 chips per second. No phrase structure. The continuity IS the structure.
song_metrics.note_types: Chip (primary, in flight), trill (excitement, near roost), squeal (alarm).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No distinct phrases. Continuous chipping during all flight activity. Rate increases near roost entry.
song_metrics.timbre: High, thin, metallic, insect-like. Almost electric. Individual chips are barely distinguishable at distance; flock creates shimmering wall of sound.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual: quiet (20-50m). Flock: moderate to loud (200m+ for large roosts). Cumulative: one swift barely audible; 10,000 = sonic event.
song_metrics.tempo: 5-10 chips per second. Extremely rapid. Increases during flock aggregation and roost entry.
song_metrics.context: All flight activity. Feeding, commuting, displaying, roosting. Inseparable from aerial lifestyle. Peak volume during communal roost entry.
song_metrics.key_property: RAPID COLLECTIVE TWITTERING -- 5000-8000 Hz, 5-10 chips/sec, cumulative flock sound. E=4. FIRST APODIDAE. Chimney roost spectacle (35,000 at Chapman). Life on the wing. Near-vestigial feet. Old-growth snags to chimneys.

plumage.overall: Dark sooty brown overall, slightly paler on throat and rump. Small size. Cigar-shaped body. Long swept-back wings. Short, spiny tail.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Cigar shape in flight. Dark overall. Rapid flickering wingbeats. Tiny size. Chimney-entering behavior in evening is diagnostic.
plumage.flight_pattern: Rapid, flickering wingbeats interspersed with gliding. Erratic, twitchy. Constant micro-adjustments. Bat-like but faster.

physical_description.overall: Length: 11-12 cm. Weight: 17-20 g. Wingspan: 27-30 cm. Very small. Among the smallest birds in the PNW. Cigar-shaped body, long wings, short spiny tail, tiny feet.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Cigar silhouette. Dark color. Rapid flight. Evening chimney swirl. Tiny size.
physical_description.field_id: Watch evening sky near chimneys or old buildings for small, dark, fast-flying birds with cigar-shaped bodies and flickering wingbeats. Listen for high-pitched chipping. The communal chimney swirl at dusk is unmistakable.

nodes.title: FIRST APODIDAE -- Swift Family Enters
nodes.description: New family. Third in four degrees (Strigidae > Caprimulgidae > Apodidae). Pure aerial specialists. 'Without feet' (apous). Swifts rarely land. Taxonomic pace accelerates.

nodes.title: Chimney Roost Spectacle
nodes.description: Up to 35,000 swifts enter one chimney (Chapman Elementary, Portland). Counterclockwise vortex. 20-45 minute entry. Community event. Chimney as old-growth substitute. Cooper's Hawks patrol.

nodes.title: Life on the Wing
nodes.description: Eats, drinks, mates in flight. Near-vestigial feet. Clings vertically to surfaces. 30-70 km/h. Cigar-shaped silhouette. Among the most aerial birds on Earth.

nodes.title: Collective Twittering -- Swarm Acoustics
nodes.description: Individual chips at 5000-8000 Hz, 5-10/second. Flock merges into continuous wall of sound. Cumulative: one swift quiet, ten thousand = sonic event. Amplitude scales with flock size.

cross_references.relationship: FAMILY TRANSITION! Caprimulgidae (146) to Apodidae (147). Both aerial insectivores. Nighthawk = crepuscular diver. Swift = pure aerial life. Increasing sky commitment.
SECOND APODIDAE! Vaux's (chimney, communal, E=4) to Black Swift (waterfall, solitary, E=4). Same family, different nesting extremes. Swift contrast pair.
OPPOSITE STRATEGIES! Poorwill (ground, torpor, E=3) vs. Swift (aerial, never lands, E=4). Ground-dweller and sky-dweller.
